sources.notes: Statement and constraints: tree game from non-leaf start v; Snorlax blocks one edge and refreshes after cooldown k.
Used to confirm the rooted tree DP returning distance to the nearest Cyndaquil-winning node and the l1 + l2 < k branching test.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate Luogu solution page; not used as evidence in this review.

annotation.summary: Decide whether Cyndaquil can force reaching a leaf in a tree while Snorlax periodically blocks one edge.
annotation.constraints: Total n <= 5e5, so the game must be characterized by a single linear DFS/tree DP from the starting vertex.
annotation.core_idea: Root the tree at v. A leaf is a direct winning target and returns distance 0. A one-child node only inherits the nearest winning distance plus one, because Snorlax can keep controlling the only direction. At a branching node, two child subtrees with nearest winning distances l1 and l2 create two threats; if l1 + l2 < k, Snorlax cannot refresh the block fast enough and the node is winning. Otherwise the node returns 1 + min child distance. The start is winning iff the root is marked winning.
tricks: root the game at the starting vertex
combine two nearest winning child distances
branching creates an unavoidable escape threat

solution_variants.name: nearest-winning-node-tree-dp
solution_variants.summary: DFS from v; each node returns either winning status or distance to the nearest winning descendant, combining the two smallest child values to test branch inevitability.

tags.evidence: The solution is a rooted tree DP whose return value summarizes each subtree by the nearest winning-state distance.
The recurrence encodes optimal play in a blocking-and-escape game, especially when a branch creates two threats Snorlax cannot cover in time.
The entire strategy relies on rooting the tree at v and combining child-subtree summaries.
A single DFS computes the two smallest child DP values at every node.
